coredata.dc:title: Electrolyte and Interface Engineering for Solid-State Sodium Batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Yong
Li, Lin
Zhang, Qiu
Niu, Zhiqiang
Chen, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Sodium batteries are considered as promising candidates for large-scale energy-storage systems owing to the abundant and low-cost sodium resources. However, many reported sodium batteries are based on conventional organic liquid electrolyte, which would lead to potential safety issues. Developing solid-state electrolyte (SSE) for sodium batteries is an effective way to solve such problems. Nevertheless, how to develop high-performance SSE and compatible interface for constructing solid-state sodium batteries is still challenging. In this review, we mainly focus on the development and recent advances of SSE (including all-solid-state and quasi-solid-state electrolyte) and interface engineering for sodium batteries. The structure-property correlations and design principles of different inorganic and organic SSE are discussed in depth. The comprehensive performance of SSE depends on the structural characteristics such as defects, crystallinity, and stability of bonds. The design principles mainly include increasing the density of mobile Na+ ions, reducing the energy barrier, immobilizing anions, adjusting the stability of bonds, adding specific buffer layers, and increasing interfacial contact area. Moreover, we discuss the interface between SSE and electrode because a suitable interface is the key prerequisite for high-performance solid-state sodium batteries. This review provides fundamental insights and future perspectives to design advanced SSE and concomitant interface for next-generation rechargeable solid-state sodium batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: sodium batteries
solid-state electrolyte
ionic conductivity
interface
safety

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Programs for Nano-Key Project ( 2017YFA0206700 ) and 111 Project from the Ministry of Education of China ( B12015 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Composition of Solid-State Sodium Batteries and the Corresponding Requirements of SSE
Crystal Structures of β-Al2O3 and β″-Al2O3
NASICON Electrolyte
(A) Crystal structures of the representative NASICON (Na3Zr2Si2PO12) with rhombohedral and monoclinic phase.
(B) Four different types of bottlenecks in Na+ ion transport pathways in the monoclinic NASICON structure. Reprinted from Park et al., 48 with permission. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society.

(C) Lattice parameter, volume of unit cell, and area of the bottleneck (marked as T1 in the inset) of Na3Zr2Si2PO12 and Na3.1Zr1.95M0.05Si2PO12 (M = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba). Reprinted from Song et al., 49 with permission. Copyright 2016, Nature Publishing Group.
(D) Average total ionic conductivity of Na3+xZr2−xScx(SiO4)2(PO4) (0 ≤ x ≤ 0.6) at 25°C. Reprinted from Ma et al., 50 with permission. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society.

(E) High-resolution elemental distribution images (obtained by synchrotron X-ray nanoprobe analysis) of La, P, Zr, and Si over the spatial regions in the Na3.3Zr1.7La0.3Si2PO12 composite electrolyte. Reprinted from Zhang et al., 51 with permission. Copyright 2016, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.
Sulfide-Based Electrolyte
(A) Crystal structures of Na3PS4 with cubic and tetragonal phase.
(B) The lowest energy ordering of the cubic Na3PS4 supercell with an Na+ interstitial. Reprinted from Zhu et al., 19 with permission. Copyright 2015, American Chemical Society.

(C) Na distribution during 100 ps at 525 K obtained by MD simulation for cubic Na3PS4 (left) and cubic Na2.94PS4 (right). Reprinted from de Klerk et al., 20 with permission. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society.

(D) The Na+ vacancy diffusion barrier in cubic Na3PSe4; inset: the structure of the initial, transition, and final states are shown in top left, bottom middle, and top right, respectively; red cross represents Na vacancy site. Reprinted from Bo et al., 61 with permission. Copyright 2015, American Chemical Society.
(E) Schematic illustration of the Na+ ion diffusion channels in Na3PS4, Na3PSe4, and Na3SbSe4. Reprinted from Wang et al., 62 with permission. Copyright 2017, Springer.

(F) Simulated and experimental XRD patterns of Na10SnP2S12, indicating the existence of recrystallized P2S5, Na2S, and Na3PS4. Reprinted from Richards et al., 63 with permission. Copyright 2016, Nature Publishing Group.

(G) Crystal structure of Na11Sn2PS12, large green spheres represent unoccupied vacancies. Reprinted from Duchardt et al., 64 with permission. Copyright 2018, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.

(H) XRD patterns of pristine Na3SbS4·9H2O, as-prepared Na3SbS4, air-exposed Na3SbS4, and reheated air-exposed Na3SbS4 (150°C for 1 hr under vacuum). Reprinted from Wang et al., 65 with permission. Copyright 2016, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.

(I) CV curves (0.05 mV s−1) for electrochemical stability window test of Na3PS4 and working window test of Na4C6O6, schematic diagram (left inset) of batteries for CV tests, and EIS (right inset) of batteries with Na3PS4/C cathode before and after the CV test. Reprinted from Chi et al., 66 with permission. Copyright 2018, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.
Complex Hydride Electrolyte
(A) Relative geometries of the B12H12 2− and B10H10 2− anions.
(B) Relative geometries of the CB11H12 − and CB9H10 − anions.

(C) Electrostatic potential surface maps (calculated by HF/3-21G level of theory) of the B12H12 2−, B12Cl12 2−, B12Br12 2−, and B12I12 2− anions. Reprinted from Hansen et al., 93 with permission. Copyright 2017, American Chemical Society.

(D and E) Simplified structure (D) and electrochemical stability window obtained by CV tests (E) of Na2(B12H12)0.5(B10H10)0.5. Reprinted from Duchêne et al., 91 with permission. Copyright 2017, The Royal Society of Chemistry.
Organic Electrolyte

(A and B) The composition (A) and LSV curve in CO2 (B) of CPE. Inset: transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of SiO2. Reprinted from Hu et al., 95 with permission. Copyright 2017, American Association for the Advancement of Science.
(C) Typical stress-strain curve of the perfluorinated sulfonic-based electrolyte. Reprinted from Hou et al., 101 with permission. Copyright 2017, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.

(D) Relationship between ionic conductivity and temperature of the PEO-NaFSI, 40 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12-PEO-NaFSI, and 40 wt% Na3.4Zr1.8Mg0.2Si2PO12-PEO-NaFSI electrolyte. Reprinted from Zhang et al., 100 with permission. Copyright 2016, The Royal Society of Chemistry.
Relationship between Ionic Conductivity and Temperature of Some Representative SSEs
Anode/Electrolyte Interface

(A) Schematic illustration and time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS) depth profiles of the Na-metal anode/Na3Zr2Si2PO12 electrolyte interface. Reprinted from Gao et al., 105 with permission. Copyright 2018, Elsevier.

(B and C) Change of polarization resistance at the Na/electrolyte interface with time for Na3PS4 and β″-Al2O3 (B), and XPS spectra of S 2p and P 2P regions of Na3PS4 before and after Na-metal deposition (C). Reprinted from Wenzel et al., 14 with permission. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society.

(D) Cycling performance of the symmetric cell (Na/Na3SbS4/Na) at room temperature, current density: 0.1 mA cm−2. Reprinted from Wang et al., 65 with permission. Copyright 2016, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.

(E) Cycling performance of the symmetric Na/Na2(B12H12)0.5(B10H10)0.5/Na cell at 60°C, current density: 100 μA cm−2. Reprinted from Duchêne et al., 91 with permission. Copyright 2017, The Royal Society of Chemistry.

(F) Resistance change of the Na/PEO-based electrolyte/Na symmetric cell with time at ambient temperature. Inset: Nyquist plots of the EIS measurements. Reprinted from Colò et al., 106 with permission. Copyright 2017, Elsevier.
Cathode/Electrolyte Interface
(A) Schematic diagram and TOF-SIMS depth profiles of the Na2MnFe(CN)6 cathode/Na3Zr2Si2PO12 electrolyte interface. Reprinted from Gao et al., 105 with permission. Copyright 2018, Elsevier.
(B) XRD patterns of the mixture of Na3PS4 and NaCrO2 at 25°C, 300°C, 500°C, and 700°C. Reprinted from Tian et al., 13 with permission. Copyright 2017, The Royal Society of Chemistry.

(C) Schematic illustration of the Na/NASICON/Na3V2(PO4)3 solid-state batteries with ionic liquid added in the cathode side. Reprinted from Zhang et al., 51 with permission. Copyright 2016, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.

(D) Schematic diagram of solid-state batteries with Na3Zr2Si2PO12 particles (top) or plastic-crystal electrolyte (bottom) in the cathode; conductive carbon particles are omitted for clarity. Reprinted from Gao et al., 109 with permission. Copyright 2017, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.

(E) TEM images and corresponding elemental mappings of Na3SbS4-coated NaCrO2 sample, initial charge/discharge curves (50 μA cm−2, 30°C), and Nyquist plots of the solid-state batteries with Na3SbS4-coated or -mixed NaCrO2 cathode. Reprinted from Banerjee et al., 15 with permission. Copyright 2016, Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.

(F and G) Schematic diagram of the synthetic process and front view of the cast-annealed Na2S-Na3PS4-CMK-3 composites (F), and cycling performance of the solid-state Na-S batteries with cast-annealed or ball-milled Na2S-Na3PS4-CMK-3 composite cathode (G). Reprinted from Fan et al., 110 with permission. Copyright 2018, American Chemical Society.
Summary of Strategies toward Enhancing the Performance, Including Ionic Conductivity, Transference Number, Stability, Mechanical Property, and Interface Issues
The applicable SSEs in each strategy are marked by specific shapes.
Summary on the Comprehensive Performance of Representative SSE for Sodium Batteries

row.entry:
high stability
high conductivity
wide electrochemical window

poor interfacial contact
expensive large-scale production

high conductivity
easy preparation
low grain boundary resistance
malleable property

flexibility
easy preparation
good interfacial contact

low conductivity
poor thermal stability/mechanical strength

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The ionic conductivities are measured at room temperature unless stated otherwise. Activation energy is omitted if it is not mentioned in the reference.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: In these columns, “✓,” “×,” and “–” represent stable/compatibility, unstable/incompatibility, and not mentioned, respectively.

head.ce:title: Electrolyte and Interface Engineering for Solid-State Sodium Batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sodium batteries are considered as promising candidates for large-scale energy-storage systems owing to the abundant and low-cost sodium resources. However, many reported sodium batteries are based on conventional organic liquid electrolyte, which would lead to potential safety issues. Developing solid-state electrolyte (SSE) for sodium batteries is an effective way to solve such problems. Nevertheless, how to develop high-performance SSE and compatible interface for constructing solid-state sodium batteries is still challenging. In this review, we mainly focus on the development and recent advances of SSE (including all-solid-state and quasi-solid-state electrolyte) and interface engineering for sodium batteries. The structure-property correlations and design principles of different inorganic and organic SSE are discussed in depth. The comprehensive performance of SSE depends on the structural characteristics such as defects, crystallinity, and stability of bonds. The design principles mainly include increasing the density of mobile Na+ ions, reducing the energy barrier, immobilizing anions, adjusting the stability of bonds, adding specific buffer layers, and increasing interfacial contact area. Moreover, we discuss the interface between SSE and electrode because a suitable interface is the key prerequisite for high-performance solid-state sodium batteries. This review provides fundamental insights and future perspectives to design advanced SSE and concomitant interface for next-generation rechargeable solid-state sodium batteries.

The recent boom in electronics and electric vehicles has raised the demand for rechargeable batteries. However, safety issues of rechargeable batteries based on liquid electrolyte such as ethers and carbonate esters are common due to the flammability of organic solvent and hazards of electrolyte leakage. In recent years, solid-state sodium batteries have attracted extensive attention because of their improved safety, considerable energy density, and low cost. Nevertheless, high-performance solid-state electrolyte and compatible interface are still absent and need to be further developed for constructing solid-state sodium batteries.

The development and recent advances of solid-state electrolyte and concomitant interface issues for sodium batteries are systematically summarized in this review. We discuss the fundamental principles in the design of solid-state electrolyte with high ionic conductivity, high transference number, and high chemical/electrochemical stability. Furthermore, we highlight the design of interface between electrolyte and electrode, which is the key challenge in developing high-performance solid-state sodium batteries. Thus, future investigations of solid-state sodium batteries should focus on anode/electrolyte/cathode interface through the combination of theoretical calculations and experimental studies. Meanwhile, large-scale production of high-performance solid-state electrolyte via a facile and scalable method with low cost is also necessary.

This review introduces the development and recent progress of different types of solid-state electrolyte for sodium batteries, including β-alumina, NASICON, sulfide-based electrolyte, complex hydrides, and organic electrolyte. In particular, the transport mechanism, ionic conductivity, ionic transference number, chemical/electrochemical stability, and mechanical properties of solid-state electrolyte for sodium batteries are summarized. Meanwhile, the interface issues of anode/electrolyte/cathode (interfacial contact and chemical compatibility) are discussed in depth with the aim of constructing high-performance solid-state sodium batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demands for energy-storage systems in many fields stimulate the booming development of rechargeable batteries beyond lithium-ion batteries. 1,2 Due to the low cost and high abundance of sodium resources, sodium batteries have attracted extensive attention in recent years. 3 Many reported sodium batteries are based on liquid electrolyte with organic solvent such as ethers and carbonate esters. The flammability of organic solvent and hazards of electrolyte leakage would bring forth potential safety issues. In contrast, solid-state sodium batteries show greater potential for large-scale applications due to their improved stability, absence of leakage, and easy-to-direct stacking. 4,5 Thus, developing suitable solid-state electrolyte (SSE) for sodium batteries is of great significance. In fact, a solid-state β-alumina electrolyte was proposed for high-temperature sodium-sulfur (Na-S) and sodium-transition metal halides (ZEBRA) batteries with molten electrodes in the 1960s and 1980s, respectively. 6,7 These battery systems have been successfully commercialized for large-scale energy-storage applications. An increasing number of other types of SSE have been developed in recent years to satisfy the wide application of solid-state sodium batteries at room temperature.

The main components of solid-state sodium batteries and the corresponding requisite issues for SSE in practical applications are shown in Figure 1 . The main characteristics that state-of-the-art SSE should possess are high ionic conductivity, high chemical stability, large electrochemical stability window, high transference number, good mechanical properties, compatible interface, ability to inhibit dendrites, high electronic resistance, environmental friendliness, and facile preparation. 8,9 Up to now the reported SSE for sodium batteries can be divided into inorganic and organic electrolyte. In addition to β-alumina, the most widely investigated inorganic SSE for sodium batteries include Na superionic conductors (NASICON), sulfides, and complex hydrides. 10 Inorganic electrolyte generally exhibits considerable ionic conductivity (> 10−4 S cm−1), high ionic transference number (close to unity), robust mechanical properties, and good thermal stability. 11 However, their intrinsic roughness and rigidity will inevitably result in poor contact with electrode materials. Compared with inorganic electrolyte, organic polymer electrolyte such as poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) shows superior contact with electrode materials. 12 Moreover, organic polymer electrolyte exhibits good film-forming property and ability to be manufactured easily. Nevertheless, organic polymer electrolyte suffers from low ionic conductivity at room temperature, poor mechanical strength, and low ionic transference number (mostly less than 0.5). 12

It should be noted that the bottleneck of solid-state sodium batteries is no longer simply developing SSE with high ionic conductivity but how to realize a good interface between SSE and electrodes. 13 Compared with liquid electrolyte, the contact area of SSE and electrode is usually insufficient. Additionally, the chemical stability between electrolyte and electrodes should be taken into account. For example, many types of sulfide SSE are unstable with Na metal. 11,14 To achieve high-performance solid-state sodium batteries, an effective interface contact and good chemical compatibility of SSE/electrode are necessary. In the past decade, more and more researchers have focused on developing solid-state sodium batteries. 15–17 However, less attention has been paid to overviewing this field. 10,11,18 Thus, a comprehensive summary of how to optimize SSE and design good electrolyte/electrode interface for solid-state sodium batteries is highly desirable.

In this review, we introduce the development and recent progress of different types of SSE for sodium batteries, including their transport mechanism, ionic conductivity, ionic transference number, chemical/electrochemical stability, and mechanical properties. We then discuss the interface issues of SSE/electrode (interfacial contact and chemical compatibility). The strategies toward improving the performance (such as ionic conductivity) of SSE and how to optimize the interface are systematically summarized. Finally, the key challenges and perspectives of constructing high-performance solid-state sodium batteries are proposed.

In this section, we discuss the transport mechanism, fundamental properties (ionic conductivity, ionic transference number, electrochemical stability), and the corresponding measurement methods of SSE in detail. In general, the mechanism of ionic transport depends on the type of SSE. Understanding the transport mechanism would be helpful in designing SSE with high ionic conductivity. The ionic transference number reflects the effective charges carried by Na+ ions in electrolyte. A high Na+ transference number is beneficial for reducing concentration polarization within batteries. Additionally, an electrochemically stable window of SSE determines the selection of electrode materials and the working voltage range of batteries.

In crystalline inorganic materials, ionic transport usually depends on the amount of mobile Na+ ions per unit volume and the structural defects. 19,20 Vacancies (missing ions) and interstitial ions are the common structural defects, which could be produced by substitution. Ionic diffusion based on the Schottky and Frenkel defects can occur through random jumps between vacancy sites, direct interstitial jumps, or interstitial-substitutional exchange. 21 The ionic conductivity in inorganic crystalline SSE follows the Arrhenius equation
(Equation 1)
σ =
σ 0
e
−
E A
/
k B
T
,
where σ is the ionic conductivity, σ 0 is the Arrhenius pre-exponential factor, T is the absolute temperature, E A is the activation energy of diffusion, and k B is the Boltzmann constant. Thus, the overall conductivity depends on diffusion energy barrier, temperature, and pre-exponential factor (including parameters such as charge-carrier density). The diffusion energy barrier usually reflects the energy required to across the bottlenecks. Increasing the concentration of mobile Na+ ions, decreasing the steric impediment, and constructing continuous diffusion way is beneficial for achieving high ionic conductivity. 22 Similar to crystalline materials, ion transport in amorphous inorganic SSE with disordered structure occurs from local sites to neighboring sites and then on a macroscopic scale. During the migration process, the mobile Na+ ions would interact with the structural skeletons whose movements are usually temperature dependent. 23

The mechanism of ionic transport in organic polymer SSE is different from that in crystalline inorganic materials. In polymer electrolyte, Na+ ions can coordinate with the polar groups (such as -O-) on segmental chains. With the movement of polymer segmental chains, Na+ ions can hop from one coordinating site to another continuously, and thus realize the transport of Na+ ions. 12 Generally, the ionic conductivity of polymer SSE follows the Vogel-Tammann-Fulcher equation (Equation 2 as follows), the Arrhenius equation (Equation 1), or both. 24
(Equation 2)
σ =
σ 0
T
− 1
/ 2
e
− B
/
(
T −
T 0
)
,
where B is the pseudo-activation energy of the conductivity and T 0 is the reference temperature (generally about 50 K below the experimental glass transition temperature). Most polymer SSE show low conductivity at room temperature due to the limited movement of their segmental chains. 12 Improving ionic conductivity of polymer SSE can be realized by decreasing the crystallinity of polymers and increasing the concentration of dissociated Na+ ions.

The ionic conductivity of SSE is generally measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The test batteries are composed of SSE and two blocking electrodes (such as stainless steel). The ionic conductivity can be calculated by
(Equation 3)
σ =
l
S
R t
,
where l and S are the thickness and cross-sectional area of SSE, respectively, and R t is the total resistance of electrolyte. For inorganic SSE, R t includes the total resistance of bulk and grain boundary.

The ionic conductivity obtained by EIS is the apparent conductivity, which includes the contribution of both cations and anions. The fraction of the current carried by Na+ ions is the so-called ionic transference number (t +), which is also an important parameter for SSE. The definition of ionic transference number is shown by
(Equation 4)
t +
=
μ +
μ +
+
μ −
,
where μ + and μ − are the current carried by cations (Na+ ions) and anions, respectively. The transference number can be measured using the steady-state current approach in the symmetric cells with non-blocking Na electrodes (Na/SSE/Na). The transference number is determined by 25
(Equation 5)
t +
=
I
ss
(
Δ V −
I 0
R 0
)
I 0
(
Δ V −
I
ss
R
ss
)
,
where I ss is the steady-state current, I 0 is the initial current, R 0 and R ss are the resistance at initial and steady state, respectively, and ΔV is the applied constant potential (generally no more than 10 mV). A larger Na+-ion transference number means more effective Na+ ionic transport. The Na+-ion transference number of inorganic SSE generally can reach close to 1. 26 In contrast, the transference number of polymer SSE is usually lower than 0.5 because Na+ ions and anions can migrate simultaneously in the opposite direction. 27

Another important parameter of SSE is their electrochemical stability window. The traditional method to characterize the electrochemical window is cyclic voltammetry (CV) or linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) using the cells composed of SSE, a Na-metal counter electrode, and a blocking working electrode. 28 In general, the current derived from the electrochemical decomposition of SSE is too small and difficult to be detected when compared with the large current caused by plating/stripping of Na metal. 13 Thus, it is better to observe the two processes (decomposition and plating/stripping) separately. In addition, to obtain the electrochemical stability window more accurately, the test should be conducted under the conditions similar to real battery systems. For example, if SSE needs to mix with cathode materials in real batteries, the working electrode should comprise both SSE and conductive carbon in the electrochemical stability window test. 13

The β-alumina electrolyte with high ionic conductivity and good thermal properties has attracted much attention since its discovery in the 1960s. 29 Subsequently, it was successfully applied as electrolyte in large-scale energy-storage systems (high-temperature Na-S and ZEBRA batteries). 30 β-Alumina has two layered crystal structures (with alternatively stacked conduction planes and spinel blocks), that is, β-Al2O3 (hexagonal; P63/mmc; a0 = b0 = 5.58 Å, c0 = 22.45 Å) and β″-Al2O3 (rhombohedral; R-3m; a0 = b0 = 5.61 Å, c0 = 33.85 Å). 29,31 The chemical composition of β-Al2O3 is Na2O(8–11)Al2O3, whereas the β″-Al2O3 with a stoichiometry of Na2O(5–7)Al2O3 exhibits more sodium content in the conduction planes (Figure 2 ), resulting in higher ionic conductivity.

Single-crystal β″-Al2O3 could exhibit ionic conductivity as high as 1 S cm−1 at 300°C. 32 In contrast, the ionic conductivity of polycrystalline β″-Al2O3 reduces to about 2.0 × 10−3 and 0.2–0.4 S cm−1 at room temperature and 300°C, respectively. 29,33,34 It is mainly due to the unavoidably high grain boundary resistance in polycrystalline β″-Al2O3. Though its ionic conductivity is high, the pure β″-Al2O3 phase is sensitive with H2O. 35,36 Moreover, pure β″-Al2O3 is not easily prepared owing to its unfavorable thermodynamic stability. The synthesized β″-Al2O3 based on traditional solid-state reaction (above 1,000°C) generally contains undesired impurities such as β-Al2O3 and NaAlO2 (around the boundaries). The β-Al2O3 derived from the evaporation of sodium sources at high temperature would decrease ionic conductivity. 37 Meanwhile, the abnormal grain growth of β″-Al2O3 yields higher ionic conductivity but poorer mechanical properties. 38 Additionally, the NaAlO2 impurity in β″-Al2O3 is sensitive with H2O and CO2 in the air. 39 Various doping stabilizers such as Li+, Mg2+, Ni2+, and Ti4+ have been added to the reaction system to prepare purer β″-Al2O3. 40–44 Unfortunately, the mechanical property of pure β″-Al2O3 is relatively low (fracture strength: ∼200 MPa). 45 Thus, the main challenges are how to control the ratio of β″-Al2O3 to realize good mechanical strength as well as high ionic conductivity and to reduce undesired side products.

NASICON materials can offer open three-dimensional channels for fast Na+ ion transport. In 1976, Goodenough et al. first proposed the NASICON-type materials Na1+xZr2SixP3−xO12 (0 ≤ x ≤ 3), which can be derived from a solid solution of NaZr2P3O12 with partial substitution of P5+ sites by Si4+. 46,47 Among Na1+xZr2SixP3−xO12 with 0 ≤ x ≤ 3, the Na3Zr2Si2PO12 (x = 2) exhibits the highest ionic conductivity of ∼10−4 and ∼10−1 S cm−1 at room temperature and 300°C, respectively. 46,47 Thus, most research on modifying NASICON materials is based on Na3Zr2Si2PO12. As shown in Figure 3 A, Na3Zr2Si2PO12 could exhibit two different phases, that is, rhombohedral (R-3c) and monoclinic (C2/c). 47 The corner-sharing SiO4/PO4 tetrahedra and ZrO6 octahedra can form three-dimensional channels for Na+-ion transport. In the rhombohedral crystal structure, there are two distinct Na+ sites (Na1 and Na2). In contrast, there are three different Na+ sites due to the split of Na2 site (Na2 and Na3) in the monoclinic structure.

In NASICON-type SSE, the Na+ ion needs to go through bottlenecks to realize transport. For example, there are four different bottleneck regions in monoclinic NASICON structure, that is, two in the Na1-Na2 and two in the Na1-Na3 channels (Figure 3B). 48 The size of bottleneck is directly related to the migration energy barrier, and thus affects the ionic conductivity. Therefore, the general method to enhance their ionic conductivity is enlarging the bottleneck size by introduction of proper substituents. For instance, Song et al. found that using appropriate aliovalent cations as substituents for Zr sites is helpful in enlarging the bottleneck size. 49 They investigated the crystal structure and ionic conductivity of the NASICONs substituted by alkaline earth ions (Na3.1Zr1.95M0.05Si2PO12, M = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba). The refined crystal structure parameters of the NASICONs in Figure 3C reveal that area of the bottleneck (marked as T1 in the inset) increase with the substitution of alkaline earth ions such as Mg2+ ions for Zr sites. The Mg2+ ions substituted compound exhibit the largest area of T1 (about 6.522 Å2). In contrast, the area of the unmodified compound is only about 5.223 Å2. The ionic conductivity of Na3.1Zr1.95Mg0.05Si2PO12 can reach 3.5 × 10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature. However, the large Ba2+ ions substituted compound exhibit narrower bottleneck than the pristine one, indicating that the ionic radius of substitutes plays an important role. In this case, Sc3+ would be the very suitable substitutes due to its similar size to Zr4+ ion (74.5 versus 72.0 pm). The reported Sc3+ substituted compound (Na3.4Zr1.6Sc0.4Si2PO12) shows total ionic conductivity of 4.0 × 10−3 S cm−1 at 25°C (Figure 3D), which is the highest value among all reported NASICONs until now. 50 Nevertheless, the high cost of Sc may restrict the large-scale applications. Other cheaper substitutes such as Nd3+ and Y3+ also have been used to enhance the ionic conductivity of NASICON due to the enlarged bottlenecks or increased concentration of Na+ ions in the lattice. 52

The approaches mentioned above are mainly focusing on enhancing the ionic transport in bulk of NASICON. The Na+ ions transport across the grain boundary also influences the total ionic conductivity of inorganic SSE. The resistance of the grain boundary can be reduced by tuning the crystallite size and adjusting the chemical composition at the grain boundary. 51,53,54 Ihlefeld et al. studied the scaling effects on the grain boundary resistance of Na1+xZr2SixP3−xO12 (0.25 ≤ x ≤ 1.0). 53 The results reveal that decreasing the annealing temperature and increasing the Si content will lead to small crystallite size, which lowers ionic conductivity of the grain boundary. In addition, Hu's group employed a self-forming strategy to synthesize La3+-doped Na3Zr2Si2PO12. 51 They found that several new phases of Na3La(PO4)2, La2O3 and LaPO4 emerged at the grain boundary (Figure 3E). The emerged new phases adjust the chemical composition of the grain boundary, contributing to fast Na+-ion migration. Some other works also demonstrate the positive effect of new phases such as Na3PO4 at the grain boundary on enhancing the ionic conductivity of the grain boundary. 54 These works pave the way to enhance the total ionic conductivity of NASICON by adjusting the Na+-ion transport at the grain boundary.

The traditional method to synthesize Na1+xZr2SixP3−xO12 (0 ≤ x ≤ 3) is solid-state reaction at high temperature (above 1,000°C). 46–48 The high processing temperature will inevitably lead to the evaporation of Na and P resources, and formation of impurities such as ZrO2. 55 Using excess Na and P resources in the reactants is helpful in mitigating this problem. 56 To realize their large-scale applications, decreasing the sintering temperature is necessary. The sol-gel approach can reduce the processing temperature to some extent owing to the uniform mixing of reactants at molecular level and decreased mass transfer path. 57 Additionally, inspired by the production of garnet-type ceramics, Suzuki et al. utilized a liquid-phase sintering method to produce Na3Zr2Si2PO12 at lower temperature (700°C) with the presence of 9.1 wt% Na3BO3 (melting point: 680°C). 58 However, the preparation of the NASICON precursor still needs a high temperature at 1,100°C.

Sulfide-based electrolyte is considered as a kind of promising electrolyte for sodium batteries due to its high ionic conductivity, moderate synthetic conditions, low grain boundary resistance, and malleable properties. 13,15,17,28 Compared with analogous oxides, the sulfide SSE generally exhibits enhanced migration of Na+ ions because the large ionic radius and high polarizability of the S atom weaken the electrostatic interactions with Na+ ions. 19 One of the most common sulfide-based SSE for sodium batteries is Na3PS4. As shown in Figure 4 A, Na3PS4 exhibits two crystal structures, that is, tetragonal (P−421c; a0 = b0 = 6.9520 Å, c0 = 7.0757 Å) and cubic (I−43m; a0 = b0 = c0 = 7.0699 Å). 59,60 In general, Na3PS4 exists in the form of a tetragonal phase, which can transform to the cubic one (high-temperature phase) at about 530 K.

In 1992, Jansen and Henseler reported the tetragonal and cubic Na3PS4, and confirmed the tetragonal structure through single-crystal X-ray diffraction (XRD). 59 The ionic conductivity of tetragonal Na3PS4 is only 4.17 × 10−6 S cm−1 (50°C), which cannot satisfy the requirement for practical applications. The investigation of sulfide SSE for sodium batteries was stagnant until Hayashi and co-workers reported the glass-ceramic sulfide electrolyte with a high ambient temperature conductivity of 2 × 10−4 S cm−1 in 2012. 28 The high ionic conductivity can be attributed to the stabilization of cubic Na3PS4 in the glass-ceramic electrolyte at ambient temperature. Furthermore, the ionic conductivity could increase to 4.6 × 10−4 S cm−1 at room temperature by improving the purity of raw Na2S. 67 Since then, sulfide-based SSE has attracted extensive attention for applications in sodium batteries.

The common way to enhance the ionic conductivity of Na3PS4 is by introducing defects (Na+ interstitials or vacancies) in the lattice. The first-principles investigations reveal that Na+ interstitials are helpful in enhancing ionic conductivity of Na3PS4 due to the increased charge-carrier density. 19
Figure 4B shows the stable structure of cubic Na3PS4 with an Na+ interstitial. The Na+ interstitials can be created by aliovalent cation doping of M4+ (M = Si, Sn, Ge) for P5+ sites. Many experimental results verified the positive effect of Na+ interstitials. For instance, the Si-doped sulfide glass-ceramic electrolyte (94Na3PS4·6Na4SiS4) showed higher ionic conductivity (7.4 × 10−4 S cm−1) than the undoped state. 68 In addition to interstitials, Na+ vacancies also have much influence on the ionic conductivity of sulfide SSE. De Klerk and Wagemaker studied the effect of Na+ vacancies on the ionic conductivity of Na3PS4 by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. 20 Compared with pristine cubic Na3PS4, the mobility of Na+ ions increases drastically in the sulfide (Na2.94PS4) with Na vacancies (Figure 4C), resulting in high ionic conductivity of 0.17 S cm−1 at 300 K. Additionally, Bo and co-workers found that the perfect stoichiometric Na3PSe4 shows negligible diffusion for Na+ ions even at 900 K via ab initio MD simulations. 61 After introduction of 2.1% Na+ vacancies, the ionic conductivity could be enhanced remarkably (theoretical value: 2.89 × 10−2 S cm−1 at 300 K). As shown in Figure 4D, the energy barrier for Na+ vacancy diffusion in Na3PSe4 is only 5 kJ mol−1 at 0 K, implying favorable Na+ ion transport. According to the balance of charge theory, Na+ vacancies can be generated by aliovalent anions (such as Cl− and Br−) doping for S2− sites. The experimental measurement shows that the ionic conductivity of Cl-doped sulfide (tetragonal Na2.9375PS3.9375Cl0.0625) is 1.14 × 10−3 S cm−1 at 303 K, which is much higher than that of pristine tetragonal Na3PS4 (5 × 10−5 S cm−1). 69

Additionally, the interactions between Na+ ions and anions’ skeleton frameworks, and the size of unit cells/channels have significant influence on the ionic conductivity. Theoretical calculations reveal that Se doping can increase the polarizability of anion frameworks and soften the lattice, resulting in reduced activation barrier. 70 Meanwhile, relevant experiments demonstrate that the fully Se-substituted electrolyte (Na3PSe4) exhibits higher ionic conductivity (1.16 × 10−3 S cm−1) than the corresponding sulfide (Na3PS4). 26 Further modification of Na3PSe4 can be realized by Sb doping for P sites to obtain higher ionic conductivity. With the increasing content of Sb, the ionic conductivity improves due to the enlarged unit cells and channels (Figure 4E). 62 In particular, the fully Sb-doped SSE (Na3SbSe4) shows high ionic conductivity of 3.7 × 10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature.

Motivated by the discovery of the superionic Li-conducting sulfide SSE with high ionic conductivity such as Li10MP2S12 (M = Si, Ge, and Sn) and Li7P3S11, many researchers have dedicated efforts to develop the analogous SSE for Na+-ion transport. 71,72 Theoretical studies reveal that the ionic conductivity of Na10GeP2S12, Na7P3S11, and Na7P3Se11 could reach as high as 4.7 × 10−3, 1.1 × 10−2, and 1.26 × 10−2 S cm−1, respectively. 73,74 Up to now, there are no reports about the successful synthesis of these pure SSEs. Recently, Ceder's group found that the ionic conductivity of superionic conductors Na10MP2S12 (M = Si, Sn, Ge) followed the order of Si > Ge > Sn by density functional theory simulations. 63 They further synthesized Na10SnP2S12 with ionic conductivity of 4 × 10−4 S cm−1. However, there is a considerable amount of impurity phases such as P2S5, Na2S, and Na3PS4 in the obtained samples (Figure 4F). Subsequently, Nazar's group successfully synthesized pure sulfide with the chemical formula Na11Sn2PS12 and confirmed its structure by single-crystal XRD and ab initio MD simulations. 17 At the same time, Duchardt and co-workers also synthesized the same SSE with high ionic conductivity of (3.7 ± 0.3) × 10−3 S cm−1, which is one of the highest values until now. 64 The high ionic conductivity could be ascribed to the presence of vacancies in the lattice (Figure 4G). The vacancies can interconnect ion transport pathway in a three-dimensional manner, which was demonstrated by theoretical calculations. It is worthwhile to make further efforts to synthesize pure Na11Sn2PS12 analogs such as Na11M2PS12 (M = Si, Ge), which are likely to exhibit higher ionic conductivity than Na11Sn2PS12. In addition, the Li7P3S11 analogs (Na7P3S11 and Na7P3Se11) are also well worth investigating by experiments.

Although sulfide-based SSE can exhibit high ionic conductivity to satisfy the demand for practical applications, they still face a huge challenge due to the fact that most types of sulfide-based SSE such as Na3PS4 are unstable in the air. 26,28 The stability of sulfide-based SSE could be enhanced by chemical encapsulation or doping. For example, in the ternary system Na2Se-Ga2Se3-GeSe2, the crystal phase encapsulated in an inert glass matrix is stable against H2O. 75 Owing to the relatively weak P-S bonds, most sulfide SSE can react with H2O and O2 to generate toxic H2S gas. 10,11,28 Thus, replacing the sensitive P-S bonds through doping is effective in enhancing the chemical stability of sulfide-based SSE. With P5+ partly replaced by As5+, the sulfide (Na3P0.62As0.38S4) exhibits enhanced moisture stability due to the stronger As-S affinity compared with P-S. 76 After full substitution by Sb, the stability of sulfide SSE (Na3SbS4) could be further enhanced. 15,65,77 Na3SbS4 is stable when exposed to O2 and H2O, which can be well explained by hard and soft acid/base theory. In Na3PS4, the S2− (soft base) tends to be replaced by O2− (hard base) due to the stronger interactions between O2− and P5+ (hard acid), while in Na3SbS4 the bonding between Sb5+ (soft acid) and S2− is much stronger and not easily broken by O2−. Additionally, Na3SbS4 can be synthesized under moderate conditions such as the aqueous-solution method. 15,65,78,79 When exposed in the air, pure Na3SbS4 tends to form Na3SbS4·xH2O and could lose H2O reversibly after heating at 150°C for 1 hr (Figure 4H). 65 Although the stability and ionic conductivity (∼10−3 S cm−1) of Na3SbS4 is attractive, the toxicity of Sb should be considered.

In addition to their chemical stability, the electrochemical stability of sulfide-based SSE is an important factor for battery applications. The common sulfide-based SSE such as Na3PS4 and Na3SbS4 exhibits a stable electrochemical window up to 5 V (versus Na+/Na) according to the CV results, obtained by using a blocking working electrode (such as stainless steel). 28,65,80 Recent works indicate that the theoretical and experimental anodic decomposition potential of the representative sulfide Na3PS4 are 2.3 and 2.7 V versus Na+/Na, respectively (Figure 4I). 13,66 In the test, the working electrode contains Na3PS4 and conductive carbon materials. Sulfide SSE is usually added in the cathode to reduce the interfacial resistance in practical battery systems. 28,66 Thus, this measurement method would be more accurate because the evaluation of the electrochemical stability window should be conducted under conditions similar to those for real batteries. Moreover, observing the decomposition process of SSE and plating/stripping of Na metal separately is preferable. It should be noted that many types of sulfide-based SSE are found to exhibit limited electrochemical stability windows when using the more precise measurement approaches. 28,66 Therefore, further studies to improve their electrochemical stability are necessary. In addition, recent studies reveal that the conductive carbon additives could stimulate the electrochemical decomposition of sulfide-based SSE (Li10GeP2S12) in all-solid-state lithium batteries during cycling processes, leading to large interfacial resistance and capacity fading. 81,82 It is meaningful to investigate whether the conductive carbon would affect the electrochemical stability and interface of sulfide-based SSE in sodium batteries.

The complex hydrides were first reported as Na+-ion solid electrolyte by Orimo and co-workers in 2012. 83 They found that the Na+ ionic transference numbers of NaAlH4 and Na3AlH6 were close to 1. Although the ionic conductivities of NaAlH4 and Na3AlH6 at room temperature were only 2.1 × 10−10 and 6.4 × 10−7 S cm−1, respectively, this study paves the way to investigate complex hydrides as SSE for sodium batteries. 83 Subsequently, they combined NaBH4 and NaNH2 with a molar ratio of 1:1 to form Na2(BH4)(NH2), which shows higher ionic conductivity (3 × 10−6 S cm−1 at 300 K) than pristine NaBH4 and NaNH2. 84 The high conductivity of Na2(BH4)(NH2) can be attributed to the specific antiperovskite-type structure with Na+ vacancies.

Compared with small anions (e.g. BH4 − and NH2 −), complex hydrides with large anions such as B12H12 2− and B10H10 2− (Figure 5 A) generally exhibit higher ionic conductivity above the temperature of order-disorder structural phase transition. 85,86 For instance, Na2B12H12 could show high conductivity (>0.1 S cm−1) at 573 K due to its high-temperature disordered body-centered-cubic phase (cation-vacancy-rich structure). 85 Nevertheless, the phase-transition temperature is too high to satisfy the requirement for practical applications in different fields. Reducing or eliminating the phase-transition temperature of complex hydride is necessary. There are three main methods to achieve this goal: chemical modification of anions, mixing anions, and crystallite nanosizing/disordering. 87–92 For example, after introducing C to modify anions of Na2B12H12 and Na2B10H10 (Figure 5B), their phase-transition temperature can be decreased dramatically (NaCB11H12 versus Na2B12H12: 380 versus 529 K; NaCB9H10 versus Na2B10H10: 290 versus 380 K). 87,88 The decreased phase-transition temperature after C substitution can be attributed to the modification of the local static cation-anion interactions, the orientational preferences, and the rotational dynamics of the anions. 89 However, when H atoms are fully replaced by halogens to form Na2B12X12 (X = Cl, Br, I), the anion size/mass and the anisotropic electron density in covalently bound halogen atoms (Figure 5C) would increase, leading to much higher phase-transition temperature than pristine Na2B12H12. 93 Recent reports reveal that mixing different anions is helpful in lowering or eliminating the transition temperature due to the introduced geometric frustrations. 90,91 The Na2(B12H12)0.5(B10H10)0.5 (Figure 5D) obtained by mixing two different anions exhibits high ionic conductivity of 9 × 10−4 S cm−1 at 20°C without obvious structural phase transition (between −70°C and 280°C). 91 In addition, Tang and co-workers found that the crystallite-size reduction and disordering realized by ball-milling are effective in lowering the transition temperature, resulting in high ionic conductivity around room temperature. 92

In addition to ionic conductivity, the chemical/electrochemical stability of hydride SSE should be considered when applying them in solid-state sodium batteries. Although Na2B10H10 tends to absorb H2O, it remains stable in the air at room temperature. 86 The electrochemical stability window of Na2(B12H12)0.5(B10H10)0.5 is about 3 V (versus Na+/Na), which was obtained by using CV to investigate the anodic decomposition process of the electrolyte separately (Figure 5E). 91 Subsequently, a stable 3-V solid-state sodium-ion battery based on this electrolyte was reported. 94 It is clear that the Na2(B12H12)0.5(B10H10)0.5 electrolyte is not suitable for cathodes with high working voltage (>3 V versus Na+/Na). Further research should pay more attention to studying the stability of complex hydrides and applying them in various solid-state sodium batteries.

Compared with inorganic SSE, organic solid electrolyte exhibits good flexibility, low interfacial contact resistance, and ability to be processed easily. Most types of Na+-ion polymer SSE are derived from the corresponding Li+-ion counterparts. The common polymer matrices for SSE are PEO, poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene) (PVDF-HFP), poly(acrylonitrile), and poly(methacrylate). 95–99 The additive salts mainly contain NaPF6, NaClO4, CF3SO3Na, NaN(SO2CF3)2, and NaN(SO2F)2(NaFSI). The main problems of most polymer electrolyte are low ionic conductivity (<10−4 S cm−1) at room temperature and unsatisfactory Na+-ion transference number (less than 0.5). 11,12 There are several approaches to handle these problems.

According to the ion transport mechanism of polymer SSE, the transport of Na+ ions depends on the movement of polymer segmental chains. 12,24 Increasing the amorphous regions is very helpful in enhancing ionic conductivity. Co-polymerization, blending, crosslinking, and combination with plasticizers are proven to be effective in achieving the goal. 12,95–102 For instance, our group fabricated quasi-SSE composed of PVDF-HFP matrix with SiO2 and NaClO4-TEGDME (tetraethylene glycol dimethyl ether) additives (Figure 6 A). 95 The ionic conductivity of the electrolyte can reach as high as 10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature. Moreover, the electrochemical stability window of the electrolyte could reach 4.5 V (Figure 6B). In fact, most polymer electrolyte exhibits a wide electrochemical stable window (>4 V versus Na+/Na). 27,95,98,100 Nevertheless, there are few reports about the application of a high-voltage cathode (>4 V) in solid-state sodium batteries with polymer electrolyte. By adding ethylene carbonate-propylene carbonate mixed solvent, the perfluorinated sulfonic-based electrolyte exhibits ionic conductivity of 2.8 × 10−4 S cm−1 at room temperature. 101 However, the mechanical strength of the electrolyte is unsatisfactory (Young's modulus: 34.78 MPa, Figure 6C). Moreover, the liquid additives in polymer electrolyte may also suffer from leakage and flammability.

In addition, many inorganic plasticizers have been developed to enhance the ionic conductivity of polymer-based SSE. The common inorganic plasticizers can be divided into two groups, inactive materials (such as SiO2, TiO2, and ZrO2) and active materials (such as NASICON). 95,96,100 The addition of these inorganic fillers can increase amorphous regions of polymer matrices and thus enhance the ionic conductivity. Moreover, the mechanical properties could also be improved at the same time. 102 For instance, the ionic conductivity of NaClO4/PEO-based electrolyte increases from 1.35 × 10−4 to 2.62 × 10−4 S cm−1 at 60°C after the addition of 5 wt% nano-sized TiO2. 96 Adding NASICON materials such as Na3Zr2Si2PO12 and Na3.4Zr1.8Mg0.2Si2PO12 also has a positive effect on the improvement of ionic conductivity. 100 As shown in Figure 6D, compared with pristine PEO-NaFSI electrolyte, ionic conductivity of the electrolyte modified by NASICON does not show a break at 40°C–50°C, implying that the addition of NASICON decreases the crystallinity of PEO. Although the addition of specific inorganic fillers can enhance the ionic conductivity of polymer-based electrolyte to some extent, their ionic conductivity still cannot satisfy the demand for room-temperature all-solid-state sodium batteries. Efforts should be made to further enhance their ionic conductivity as well as mechanical strength.

The low Na+-ion transference number of polymer SSE is attributed to the simultaneous transport of both Na+ ions and anions. 12 Although the addition of inorganic fillers could improve the transference number of Na+ ions to some extent, 103 the ultimate way to achieve this goal would be by immobilizing anions. For example, the transference number of the composite SSE can reach 0.9 after attaching the anions directly to the inorganic part of the SSE by employing silane with the sodium phosphonate groups. 104 Developing other types of single-ion polymer electrolyte is well worth trying in the future.

In this section, we systematically discussed the development and recent progress of different types of Na+-ion SSE, including β-alumina, NASICON, sulfide-based electrolyte, complex hydrides, and organic electrolyte. To realize the practical applications of these types of electrolyte in solid-state sodium batteries, one should firstly consider their intrinsic properties such as ionic conductivity and chemical/electrochemical stability. To achieve an unambiguous comparison of different types of SSE, we summarize the ionic conductivity of representative SSE for sodium batteries (Figure 7 ). Among all reported SSE for sodium batteries, optimized complex hydrides show the highest ionic conductivity (10−2 S cm−1) at room temperature. The ionic conductivity of β″-Al2O3, NASICON, and sulfide-based SSE can reach 10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature after optimization. Most pristine organic SSE-like polymer exhibits low ionic conductivity (<10−5 S cm−1), which can be improved by adding plasticizers (inorganic fillers or solvent).

Up to now, most works focus on maximizing the ionic conductivity of SSE. In fact, the interface issue is also an enormous challenge for the development of solid-state sodium batteries. The interface issues include chemical compatibility and interfacial contact. The interfacial contact between inorganic SSE and electrode is often insufficient. Due to the malleable property of sulfide-based and complex hydride SSE, the good contact between electrolyte and electrode is possible through simple cold pressing. 28,66,94 In contrast, β-alumina and NASICON electrolyte usually require hot pressing to achieve good contact because of their brittle properties. 33,105 In this section, we mainly focus on the chemical stability of SSE toward anode/cathode materials, and how to reduce the interfacial resistance between SSE and electrodes.

The schematic diagram of the interface between NASICON electrolyte (Na3Zr2Si2PO12) and Na-metal anode is shown in Figure 8 A. 105 Na3Zr2Si2PO12 would react with Na metal to form a thin passivation layer (represented by NaO−, Figure 8A), which can function as a stable interphase to prevent electrolyte from further reactions with Na metal. Moreover, the passivation film is help to enhance the wettability of Na-metal anode, resulting in improved interfacial contact between electrolyte and anode. In contrast, there are no side reactions between β″-Al2O3 and Na metal, which can be concluded from the fact that the polarization resistance at the Na/β″-Al2O3 interface does not change with time (Figure 8B). 14

Compared with β″-Al2O3, the most common sulfide electrolyte (Na3PS4) is not stable after contacting with Na metal. As shown in Figure 8B, the polarization resistance at the Na/Na3PS4 interface increases obviously with time. 14 The X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) spectra of Na3PS4 before and after Na-metal deposition in Figure 8C reveal that the decomposition of Na3PS4 leads to the formation of side products (Na2S and Na3P). Thus, the anodes of many reported batteries based on Na3PS4 electrolyte are Na-Sn alloys instead of Na metals. 28,66 After full substitution of P5+ sites by Sb5+, the obtained Na3SbS4 is stable with Na metal, which can be proven by the stable Na plating and stripping in the Na/Na3SbS4/Na symmetric cell (Figure 8D). 65 The reason why Na3SbS4 is stable with Na metal still requires study. Recently, theoretical investigations reveal that introducing buffer materials such as HfO2, Sc2O3, and ZrO2 between sulfide SSE and Na metal can improve the interface issues. 107 Although some progress has been made, further works still should pay more attention to enhancing the stability of sulfide SSE with Na metal to facilitate their practical application in solid-state Na-metal batteries. Unlike sulfide SSE, the representative hydride electrolyte, Na2(B12H12)0.5(B10H10)0.5, remains stable after contacting with Na metal. 91 The overpotential of the symmetric cell that cycled at 0.1 mA cm−2 remained stable for 12 days (Figure 8E), indicating that the Na2(B12H12)0.5(B10H10)0.5 electrolyte is not degrading.

In general, polymers such as PEO would react with Na metal slightly to form passivation layers. 103,106 As shown in Figure 8F, the resistance of the symmetric cell (Na/SSE/Na) with PEO-based electrolyte initially increases and finally remains stable, implying that there may exist a stable passivation layer between Na metal and electrolyte, which inhibits further reactions. 106 The formation mechanism and property of passivation layers between Na metal and polymer SSE need to be further investigated. It should be noted that succinonitrile, a common plastic-crystal electrolyte, is unstable with Na metal because active Na metal could catalyze the polymerization of C≡N. 108

In addition to the Na/electrolyte interface, the chemical stability and interfacial contact of cathode/electrolyte are also important. Figure 9 A shows the interface between Na2MnFe(CN)6 cathode and NASICON electrolyte (Na3Zr2Si2PO12). 105 A thin passivation layer would form at the interface, which was demonstrated by time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS). In addition, the chemical stability between cathode and SSE can be investigated by other approaches such as XRD, XPS, and differential scanning calorimetry. Through these methods, Ceder's group studied the chemical stability between sulfide-based SSE Na3PX4 (X = S, Se) and common layered transition metal oxide cathode (NaCrO2). 13 The results reveal that NaCrO2 remains stable with Na3PX4 below 300°C (Figure 9B). The chemical stability between the discharge/charge intermediates of cathode materials and SSE should also be considered.

Compared with chemical compatibility of cathode/SSE, more attention has been paid to enhancing the interfacial contact between cathode and SSE. The most common way to improve the cathode/electrolyte contact is by mixing SSE with active materials during the preparation process of electrode. 51,66 For inorganic SSE, this method is generally not very effective due to their rigid particles. Introducing additional liquid solvent such as ionic liquid could further reduce the interfacial resistance. 51,111 As shown in Figure 9C, the Na+-ion transport could be enhanced after the addition of N-methyl-N-propylpiperidinium-bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide, resulting in dramatically enhanced rate capability and cycling performance. 51 Adding plastic-crystal electrolyte in the cathode is another effective way to enhance the interfacial contact. Goodenough's group used a plastic-crystal electrolyte (NaClO4/succinonitrile), which could deform reversibly with cathode-particle volume change to reduce the interfacial resistance in the cathode (Figure 9D). 109 The interfacial resistance on the cathode side with NaClO4/succinonitrile was only 370.6 Ω after 100 cycles, which was much lower than that with Na3Zr2Si2PO12 electrolyte after one cycle (9,792.7 Ω). However, the toxicity and low melting point (about 56°C) of succinonitrile may restrict its practical application.

If SSE (such as polymer) can be dissolved in some proper solvent, casting electrolyte on the cathode or mixing electrolyte/active materials in solution followed by evaporation of solvent to form integrated cathode will be very beneficial for reducing the interfacial resistance. 15,97 For example, due to the high solubility of Na3SbS4 in methanol, the coating of Na3SbS4 electrolyte (about 200 nm thick) on NaCrO2 cathode (Figure 9E) could be realized by the solution-based coating approach. 15 As shown in Figure 9E, the Na3SbS4-coated NaCrO2 cathode could exhibit much higher discharge capacity (108 mAh g−1) than the simple mixed electrode (3 mAh g−1). Moreover, the charge-transfer resistance at the Na3SbS4/NaCrO2 interface is very small compared with the simple mixed electrode (0.6 versus 30 kΩ). Nevertheless, the ionic conductivity of Na3SbS4 decreases from 1.1 × 10−3 to 1–3 × 10−4 S cm−1 after using this solution-based coating method due to the change of crystallinity, formation of impurities, or both.

Another effective way to reduce the cathode/electrolyte interfacial resistance is in situ preparation of composites of SSE and active materials. 16,110 For instance, Wang's group fabricated a Na2S-Na3PS4-CMK-3 composite cathode for solid-state Na-S batteries through an in situ casting-annealing method, whereby the Na2S was synthesized from Na3PS4 (Figure 9F). 110 Compared with ball-milled Na2S-Na3PS4-CMK-3, the in situ cast-annealed cathode exhibited higher capacity and better cycling stability (Figure 9G) due to the intimate contact among Na2S, Na3PS4 solid electrolyte, and highly conductive CMK-3. However, the content of Na2S in the composite cathode is only 30 wt%, which inevitably affects the whole energy density of the batteries. In addition to the approaches mentioned above, decreasing the particle size of active materials or construction of batteries with a single component also have been proved effective in reducing the interfacial resistance. 112–114

In conclusion, this review provides an overview of different types of SSE and concomitant interface issues for sodium batteries. Among all reported SSE, only β-alumina has been successfully commercialized in high-temperature Na-S and ZEBRA batteries with molten electrodes. However, it is challenging to apply β-alumina in sodium batteries at room temperature due to the poor interfacial contact between electrolyte and active materials. In addition to β-alumina, other inorganic SSE also suffer from some obstacles, which hinders their practical applications in room-temperature sodium batteries. The challenges include the rigorous synthetic conditions of NASICON electrolyte, the poor chemical/electrochemical stability of sulfide-based SSE, and the limited electrochemical stability of complex hydride SSE. Moreover, the interfacial issues (interfacial contact and chemical compatibility) of all inorganic SSE need to be addressed properly. For organic SSE, the relatively low ionic conductivity, limited thermal stability, and poor mechanical strength inevitably impede their application for sodium batteries. At present, the ionic conductivity of different types of optimized SSE could satisfy the demand for applications (>10−4 S cm−1). Therefore, the performance of solid-state sodium batteries is mainly dominated by the electrolyte/electrode interfaces. In this regard, sulfide-based and polymer SSE would be the promising alternatives for practical applications because good interfacial contact between them and electrodes could be achieved without hot pressing. Thus, how to enhance the chemical/electrochemical stability of sulfide-based SSE and improve the room-temperature ionic conductivity of polymer SSE is significant.

Combining the literature and our understanding, the fundamental principles to design advanced SSE and concomitant interface are summarized as follows. (1) To design SSE with high ionic conductivity, increasing the density of mobile Na+ ions and reducing the energy barrier is necessary because ionic conductivity is closely related to the charge-carrier density (mobile Na+ ions) and diffusion energy barrier (the required energy to cross the bottlenecks). (2) Immobilizing the anions is helpful in improving the ionic transference number of polymer SSE. (3) Adjusting the strength of bonds in SSE could enhance the chemical stability. For instance, the chemical stability of sulfide-based SSE can be tuned based on hard and soft acid/base theory. (4) Adding specific buffer layers and designing appropriate structure of SSE is helpful in enhancing the interfacial stability. (5) Sufficient contact area between SSE and electrode during discharge/charge processes is necessary to achieve low interfacial resistance. For example, a large interfacial contact area between sulfide-based SSE and electrode could be realized by cold pressing. (6) Toxic or expensive elements are not suitable to be used in electrolyte if considering the environmental issue and cost.

To realize practical applications of solid-state sodium batteries at room temperature, we suggest that further studies focus on the following aspects. First, it is important to design SSE with high performance (such as high ionic conductivity and good chemical/electrochemical stability) based on the fundamental principles and theoretical calculations. Second, optimizing the comprehensive performance (such as ionic conductivity and stability) of SSE and interface through experimental studies is meaningful. For example, the chemical/electrochemical stability of sulfide-based SSE can be improved by elemental substitution. Third, more advanced in situ techniques (such as XRD, XPS, and TEM) should be adopted to characterize SSE and interface in solid-state sodium batteries. Finally, how to realize large-scale production of high-performance SSE via a facile and scalable method with low cost is the prerequisite for practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Programs for Nano-Key Project (2017YFA0206700) and 111 Project from the Ministry of Education of China (B12015).

ce:section.ce:para: J.C. proposed the topic of the review. Y.L. and L.L. conducted the literature search. Q.Z. designed the figures. J.C., Z.N., Y.L., L.L., and Q.Z. discussed, wrote, and revised the manuscript.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Kummer, J.T., and Weber, N. (1968). Battery having a molten alkali metal anode and a molten sulfur cathode. US patent 3413150.

Lithium battery chemistries enabled by solid-state electrolytes
Electrochemical energy storage with mediator-ion solid electrolytes
Progress in the development of sodium-ion solid electrolytes
Recent advances in solid polymer electrolytes for lithium batteries
